source_paper_title: Learning and Balancing Unknown Loads in Large-Scale Systems

context:
Page 21, Section 4.3 (Extensions)

It is also possible to prove that (8b) and (8c) hold if the hypotheses of Theorem 3 hold, except that the basic learning scheme is replaced by the refined learning scheme. ... Nevertheless, proving that the threshold eventually reaches an equilibrium in all sufficiently large systems would require non-trivial additional arguments. In particular, the arguments used in Section 5.3 to establish this property in the case of exponentially distributed service times cannot be easily extended to a scenario with general Coxian service times.
title: Equilibrium of refined learning threshold with Coxian service times setting
background_latex:
Consider a system of \(n\) identical server pools, each with infinitely many servers, so that tasks at a given pool are processed in parallel and a task’s service requirement is unaffected by the number of concurrent tasks. Tasks arrive according to a Poisson process of rate \(n\lambda\) (constant in time) and are dispatched immediately upon arrival to one server pool according to a threshold-based rule.

Service times are Coxian (phase-type) with at most \(r\) exponential phases: a task starts in phase \(1\), completes phase \(m\) after an exponential time with mean \(1/\mu_m\), and then either advances to phase \(m+1\) with probability \(p_m\) or departs the system with probability \(1-p_m\), with \(p_r=0\). The mean service time is \(1/\mu\), and the offered load per pool is \(\rho:=\lambda/\mu\).

Let \(x_i(t)\in\mathbb{N}\) be the total number of tasks at pool \(i\) at time \(t\). Define the occupancy tail fractions
\[q_n(t,i):=\frac{1}{n}\#\{\text{pools with at least } i \text{ tasks at time } t\},\qquad i\ge 0,
\]
so \(q_n(t,0)=1\) and \(q_n(t,i)\) is nonincreasing in \(i\). The normalized total number of tasks is
\[u_n(t):=\sum_{i\ge 1} q_n(t,i)=\frac{N_n(t)}{n},\]
where \(N_n(t)\) is the total number of tasks in the system.

Fix an integer parameter \(\Delta\ge 1\). The dispatching rule uses a threshold \(\ell_n(t)\in\{0,\Delta,2\Delta,\dots\}\) and an upper level \(h_n(t)=\ell_n(t)+\Delta\): arrivals are routed uniformly at random to (i) a pool with fewer than \(\ell_n(t)\) tasks if any exist; else (ii) a pool with between \(\ell_n(t)\) and \(h_n(t)-1\) tasks if any exist; else (iii) any pool (all have at least \(h_n(t)\) tasks).

A refined learning scheme updates \(\ell_n(t)\) at certain arrival epochs based on partial occupancy information (fractions of pools above/below threshold). The question concerns whether, under Coxian service times, \(\ell_n(t)\) stabilizes to an equilibrium value (typically \(\lfloor \rho\rfloor_\Delta\), the greatest multiple of \(\Delta\) below \(\rho\)) in large systems.
problem_definition_latex: Establish conditions under which, for Coxian service times with constant arrival rate \(n\lambda\), the threshold process \(\ell_n(t)\) generated by the refined learning scheme converges (in the sense that for some deterministic \(\ell^*\in\{0,\Delta,2\Delta,\dots\}\) and some \(\sigma>0\), \(\ell_n(t)=\ell^*\) for all \(t\ge \sigma\) and all sufficiently large \(n\) with probability one) to an equilibrium value, and characterize \(\ell^*\) in terms of \(\rho\) and \(\Delta\).
latex:
Consider a system of \(n\) identical server pools, each with infinitely many servers, so that tasks at a given pool are processed in parallel and a task’s service requirement is unaffected by the number of concurrent tasks. Tasks arrive according to a Poisson process of rate \(n\lambda\) (constant in time) and are dispatched immediately upon arrival to one server pool according to a threshold-based rule.

Service times are Coxian (phase-type) with at most \(r\) exponential phases: a task starts in phase \(1\), completes phase \(m\) after an exponential time with mean \(1/\mu_m\), and then either advances to phase \(m+1\) with probability \(p_m\) or departs the system with probability \(1-p_m\), with \(p_r=0\). The mean service time is \(1/\mu\), and the offered load per pool is \(\rho:=\lambda/\mu\).

Let \(x_i(t)\in\mathbb{N}\) be the total number of tasks at pool \(i\) at time \(t\). Define the occupancy tail fractions
\[q_n(t,i):=\frac{1}{n}\#\{\text{pools with at least } i \text{ tasks at time } t\},\qquad i\ge 0,
\]
so \(q_n(t,0)=1\) and \(q_n(t,i)\) is nonincreasing in \(i\). The normalized total number of tasks is
\[u_n(t):=\sum_{i\ge 1} q_n(t,i)=\frac{N_n(t)}{n},\]
where \(N_n(t)\) is the total number of tasks in the system.

Fix an integer parameter \(\Delta\ge 1\). The dispatching rule uses a threshold \(\ell_n(t)\in\{0,\Delta,2\Delta,\dots\}\) and an upper level \(h_n(t)=\ell_n(t)+\Delta\): arrivals are routed uniformly at random to (i) a pool with fewer than \(\ell_n(t)\) tasks if any exist; else (ii) a pool with between \(\ell_n(t)\) and \(h_n(t)-1\) tasks if any exist; else (iii) any pool (all have at least \(h_n(t)\) tasks).

A refined learning scheme updates \(\ell_n(t)\) at certain arrival epochs based on partial occupancy information (fractions of pools above/below threshold). The question concerns whether, under Coxian service times, \(\ell_n(t)\) stabilizes to an equilibrium value (typically \(\lfloor \rho\rfloor_\Delta\), the greatest multiple of \(\Delta\) below \(\rho\)) in large systems.

Establish conditions under which, for Coxian service times with constant arrival rate \(n\lambda\), the threshold process \(\ell_n(t)\) generated by the refined learning scheme converges (in the sense that for some deterministic \(\ell^*\in\{0,\Delta,2\Delta,\dots\}\) and some \(\sigma>0\), \(\ell_n(t)=\ell^*\) for all \(t\ge \sigma\) and all sufficiently large \(n\) with probability one) to an equilibrium value, and characterize \(\ell^*\) in terms of \(\rho\) and \(\Delta\).
open_problem_quote: It is also possible to prove that (8b) and (8c) hold if the hypotheses of Theorem 3 hold, except that the basic learning scheme is replaced by the refined learning scheme. ... Nevertheless, proving that the threshold eventually reaches an equilibrium in all sufficiently large systems would require non-trivial additional arguments. In particular, the arguments used in Section 5.3 to establish this property in the case of exponentially distributed service times cannot be easily extended to a scenario with general Coxian service times.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The statement is existential/universal about almost-sure stabilization in the many-server limit under a learning rule, and failure may depend on rare-event dynamics; searching finite counterexamples is unlikely to be decisive because behavior can change with scaling in n and with distributional parameters.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: One can simulate the refined learning scheme for Coxian parameters and large n and use a fitness signal measuring frequency/amplitude of threshold oscillations, potentially suggesting conjectures or identifying problematic parameter regimes. However, translating simulation evidence into a proof of almost-sure equilibrium remains nontrivial and the space of Coxian parameters is large.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The Coxian-service version of the equilibrium question is essentially resolved in the 2024 paper "Learning and balancing unknown loads in large-scale systems". In the constant-arrival Coxian setting, the authors analyze the threshold-based dispatching rule with a learning outer loop and prove eventual stabilization of the threshold when the threshold is updated via the basic estimator \(\ell_n(t)=\lfloor N_n(t)/n\rfloor_\Delta\). Under the nondegeneracy condition \(\rho\notin\{i\Delta\}\), Theorem 3 yields a pathwise large-\(n\) statement: there exists \(\sigma>0\) such that for all sufficiently large \(n\), \(\ell_n(t)=\ell^*\) for all \(t\ge \sigma\) almost surely, with \(\ell^*=\lfloor\rho\rfloor_\Delta\). The same theorem also quantifies the resulting near-balance of the occupancy tails: \(q_n(t,i)\to 1\) for all \(i\le \ell^*\) and the excess tail mass beyond \(\ell^*+\Delta\) decays at least exponentially fast at rate bounded below by a hazard-rate constant \(\eta\).

Methodologically, the paper avoids a full fluid limit for the detailed Coxian state descriptor \(s_n(j_1,\dots,j_r)\) and instead proves strong approximations and dynamical inequalities for aggregate “mass” functionals (total mass and tail masses). This is crucial because Coxian service induces heterogeneous residual times across phases, making standard low-dimensional fluid closures difficult. The earlier paper "Utility maximizing load balancing policies" provides partial progress in the exponential case for a related self-learning threshold mechanism, establishing convergence of a learned threshold index and global stability of the limiting equilibrium; however it does not treat phase-type service.

A remaining gap relative to the user-stated open problem is the refined learning scheme under Coxian service times (where threshold updates are triggered by partial occupancy fractions rather than by \(N_n(t)\)). The 2024 paper explicitly notes that proving eventual equilibrium for the refined scheme with Coxian service would require additional nontrivial arguments, even though tail-balance bounds analogous to Theorem 3 can be obtained once one has an upper bound \(\ell_n(t)\le \lfloor\rho\rfloor_\Delta\) after some time. Thus, for Coxian service times the equilibrium characterization \(\ell^*=\lfloor\rho\rfloor_\Delta\) is established for the basic learning scheme, while the refined-scheme Coxian stabilization remains an open extension.

sources.title: Utility maximizing load balancing policies
sources.theorem_or_result: Assuming exponential service times, the paper proves that its Self-Learning Threshold Assignment (SLTA) policy’s learned threshold index \(r_n(t)\) converges to a deterministic optimal index \(r^*\) (equivalently, the induced thresholds converge to their optimal values) and that the stationary occupancy state \(q_n\) converges in \(\ell_1\) to a deterministic equilibrium \(q^*\); specifically, Theorem 2(b) shows \((q_n,r_n)\Rightarrow (q^*,r^*)\) in \(\ell_1\times\mathbb N\), and Theorem 6 gives a pathwise large-\(n\) stabilization: for \(T\ge \tau>\tau_{eq}(s_0)\), \(\sup_{t\in[\tau,T]}|r_n(t)-r^*|\to 0\) and \(q_n(t)\) is close to \(q^*\) on \([\tau,T]\).
sources.connection: The open problem asks for equilibrium/stabilization of a refined learning threshold \(\ell_n(t)\) under Coxian (multi-phase) service times. This paper analyzes a closely related self-learning threshold scheme (SLTA) and proves eventual stabilization of the learned threshold parameter (and convergence of the occupancy state to an equilibrium) but only under exponential service times (Markovian \(M/M/\infty\) dynamics). It does not treat Coxian/phase-type service times, so it does not resolve the open problem as stated. However, it provides the main proof architecture (ergodicity/tightness, nonstandard fluid approximation for learning-triggered updates, and identification of the limiting equilibrium \(r^*\)) that would be the natural starting point for extending to Coxian service times.
